Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (supporting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "-qq]]" to "-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]]"
revision.text:
'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and supply management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}

The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (supporting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.

Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (supporting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

revision.text:
'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and supply management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (supporting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (supporting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and supply management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (supporting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and supply management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (supporting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}}

'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, qqth day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September qq, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and suqqly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (suqqorting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, qqth day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September qq, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and suqqly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (suqqorting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, qqth day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September qq, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and suqqly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (suqqorting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:SuQqqq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, qqth day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September qq, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and suqqly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (suqqorting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[Minbu-shō notes]] * [[Kaisei-gakari]] * [[Modern Japanese Bureaucracy]]

Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and su11ly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

revision.text:
'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and su11ly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[Minbu-shō notes]] * [[Kaisei-gakari]] * [[Modern Japanese Bureaucracy]]

| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

revision.text:
'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and su11ly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[Minbu-shō notes]] * [[Kaisei-gakari]] * [[Modern Japanese Bureaucracy]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and su11ly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.
== References ==
=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}}== See Also == * [[Minbu-shō notes]] * [[Kaisei-gakari]] * [[Modern Japanese Bureaucracy]]

revision.text:
'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and su11ly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and su11ly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2年07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.

revision.text:
'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and su11ly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2nd year 07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.
== References ==

revision.text:
'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and su11ly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2nd year 07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.
== References ==
=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Minbu-shō notes]] * [[Kaisei-gakari]] * [[Modern Japanese Bureaucracy]]

The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.

Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and su11ly management.

Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''

Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.

'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. revision.text:
'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and su11ly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2nd year 07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.
== References ==
=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Minbu-shō notes]] * [[Kaisei-gakari]] * [[Modern Japanese Bureaucracy]]
{{citation needed|Minbu-sho}}

<!--Ministry of Popular Affairs (Meiji period)-->

The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.
== References ==
=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[Minbu-shō notes]] * [[Kaisei-gakari]] * [[Modern Japanese Bureaucracy]]
{{citation needed|Minbu-sho}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Minbu-shō''' (民部省) was one of the government ministries established during the [[Meiji era]] in Japan under the [[Dajōkan]] system. It was created on [[August 15, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[7th month, 8th day (old calendar)]]) by restructuring the [[Minbu-kan]] under the [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]]. The ministry oversaw domestic administrative affairs. On [[September 16, 1869]] (Meiji 2, [[8th month, 11th day (old calendar)]]), it merged with the [[Ōkura-shō]] (Ministry of Finance), combining its roles and headquarters into the "Minbu and Ōkura-shō" (民部・大蔵省)<ref name=mof100/>.
In [[1870]], the two ministries were separated again on [[August 6]] (Meiji 3, [[7th month, 10th day (old calendar)]]), with Minbu-shō assuming new roles such as overseeing regional taxation and infrastructure projects. However, the ministry was finally dissolved on [[September 11, 1871]] (Meiji 4, [[7th month, 27th day (old calendar)]]) and its functions absorbed into Ōkura-shō.
== Responsibilities == Upon its establishment, Minbu-shō was responsible for the following tasks: {{Quote| Minbu-shō Tasked with managing census records, taxation, postal services, mining, and social welfare, including poor relief and elderly care. Ōkura-shō Tasked with managing financial transactions, stipends, minting, construction, and su11ly management.
| [[Shokuinrei (Dajōkan)|Shokuinrei]] (Meiji 2)<ref name=shokuin>{{Cite|和書|title=官制改定職員令ヲ頒ツ |date=明治2nd year 07月08日 |author=太政官 |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/item/1341702 |publisher=National Archives of Japan}}</ref><ref name=mof100 />}}
Following its separation from Ōkura-shō in 1870, Minbu-shō expanded its responsibilities to include the following bureaus and divisions: * '''Geography Bureau (地理司)''' * '''Public Works Bureau (土木司)''' * '''Postal Services Bureau (駅逓司)''' * '''Mining Bureau (鉱山司)''' * '''General Affairs Bureau (庶務司)'''
Additional sub-departments included: * '''Litigation Office (聴訟掛)''' * '''Shrines and Temples Office (社寺掛)''' * '''Railways Office (鉄道掛)''' * '''Telegraph Office (電信機掛)''' * '''Lighthouse Office (燈明台掛)''' * '''Yokosuka Steelworks Office (横須賀製鉄所掛)'''
== Historical Background == The ministry was established during a period of intense centralization under the Meiji government. The separation of taxation (Minbu-shō) and fiscal policy (Ōkura-shō) reflected internal conflicts between the [[Saigō faction]] (advocating gradual reform) and the [[Kido faction]] (su11orting rapid modernization).
Key events include: * '''1869''': Initial merger of Minbu-shō and Ōkura-shō to centralize tax and financial administration. * '''1870''': Re-separation due to tensions between regional officials and central administrators over tax reforms. * '''1870–1871''': Establishment of specialized offices such as the Shrines and Temples Office to handle specific administrative tasks. * '''1871''': Dissolution and full absorption into Ōkura-shō following the abolition of the han system.
== Notable Officials == The following prominent figures held leadership roles: * '''Minbu-kyō (民部卿)''': [[Matsudaira Yoshinaga]] (initial head of the ministry)<ref name=shokuin />. * '''Minbu-daisuke (民部大輔)''': [[Hirozawa Motomi]]<ref name=shokuin />.
== Legacy and Abolition == Minbu-shō was abolished as part of broader administrative reforms in 1871, which sought to streamline government functions and address regional concerns about taxation and infrastructure. Its dissolution marked a significant step toward modernizing Japan's central bureaucracy, with its responsibilities folded into the Ōkura-shō.
== References ==
=== Notes === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === Citations === {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * [[Minbu-shō notes]] * [[Kaisei-gakari]] * [[Modern Japanese Bureaucracy]]
{{citation needed|Minbu-sho}}
